An Indian tech founder posted a thread on a Sunday morning that AI was going to wipe out half of Bangalore's coding jobs inside twelve months. The thread named TCS, Infosys, and Wipro. Any engineer without his own Claude agent by January was going to be unemployed by March.

Forty thousand engineers in Bangalore opened the thread before Monday standups. Twenty thousand spent the rest of the day on YouTube. The other twenty thousand updated their LinkedIn to "AI Engineer" before lunch.

A 24 year old engineer at the back of one of those offices opened the thread at 11pm on a Tuesday. He set his phone on a tripod and filmed himself learning Go. Posted forty seconds as a reply.

By Friday his own company had rebuilt the careers page around the clip. The CEO mentioned him on the earnings call. TCS sent the clip to twelve thousand internal engineers as the official response to the founder's thread.

While the West argued whether AI would take coding jobs, Bangalore was being told the answer was a forty second clip of a kid learning Go after midnight.

Every founder who had been called out in the original thread DM'd him. Every recruiter saved the clip. Every engineering student in India sent it to their parents. He never replied.

Then someone wrote: pause at 0:27. The Go installation page is real. Look at the curved Dell monitor above his head.

That second screen is not running Go documentation. That is a live wallet.

gabagool22. $868,862 profit. 28,620 entries. Joined October 2025. 735K views on the page.

https://t.co/XrVYCkVBpI

He posted forty seconds to prove the founder's prediction was wrong. The screen behind him had been quietly running the founder's prediction for four months.

28,620 positions. All BTC. All fifteen minute windows. All green. Biggest single win: $4,696. From a window that closed before he opened his work laptop.

Comments turned into a detective board. Someone slowed the video to 0.25x. Someone else zoomed the curved monitor. A third guy reconstructed the wallet page from twelve seconds of background footage.

Entry prices between 2 and 10 cents. Payouts in the thousands. Not one red row across 28,620 entries.

The Go tutorial was not the upskilling. The Go tutorial was the cover.

Six months ago a Chinese developer in Shenzhen filmed a tutorial and accidentally showed the same wallet on his second monitor. 400K views before he deleted it. The Bangalore dev was one of the forks.

The founder quoted the kid's clip three times as proof his prediction was wrong. He never opened the second tab. Neither did anyone who shared it.

The Claude agent runs BTC fifteen minute windows around the clock. Entry at 2 cents. Exit at 10. Done in fifteen. It never sleeps. It made $4,696 on the Tuesday the founder retweeted his clip.

His salary was $32K a year. The wallet made $868K in four months.

He posted forty seconds of learning Go to prove the founder's prediction was wrong. The founder's prediction was running on his second monitor the whole time. The whole industry cheered him for it.

A 14 year old Chinese schoolboy entered his middle school programming contest with an AI agent. The judges gave him third place. The certificate said the project was creative but had no real world application. He taped the certificate to his bedroom wall and pushed the same code to a public GitHub repo that night.

While America still debates whether AI is going to replace junior engineers, a fourteen year old Chinese schoolboy open sourced the agent that does it. README in broken English. Description: ai agent solves codeforces fast for me. Zero stars on day one.

Six months later his CS teacher opened the GitHub profile to grade a class assignment. The repo with no real world application had 3,100 forks. The teacher clicked through to one of them. What loaded was not a fork.

Bro pause at 0:38. Ignore the kid in the Brooklyn t-shirt. Look at the contributor list on the GitHub page.

That contributor is fourteen years old. The wallet attached to one of the forks is at $404,394.

A Singapore developer cloned the repo three weeks after upload. Swapped the Codeforces plugin for one that reads sports odds. Pointed Claude at Asian sportsbook lines. Walked away. Sixty thousand entries later the wallet sat at four hundred thousand dollars.

The detective board formed inside an hour. Someone slowed his demo to 0.25x. Someone else opened the original repo and pulled the contributor history. A third guy traced the user's other commits to a school contest entry from Shenzhen Number 4 Middle School.

The same username the judges had stamped 'no real world application' on.

60,837 entries. All sports. Across six leagues. NFL. Premier League. Champions League. La Liga. Ligue 1. NHL. Every line green. Biggest single win: $6,784. From a football match that closed in fifteen minutes.

The repo is not the product. The repo is the noise.

The real edge is what the kid understood and the judges missed. Asian sportsbooks move lines two to three hours before Western platforms. Shanghai moves before London. Beijing moves before New York. By the time American desks open the laptops, the gap is closed. The Claude agent catches every one while the West sleeps.

The kid wrote it over winter break. The Singapore developer cloned it for free. The market gave back four hundred thousand dollars in six months.

The kid still has not made a dollar from his own repo. GitHub's terms say he cannot sell anything yet. He is too young.

The teacher who opened the profile to grade homework closed the laptop and called the principal instead.

The school said no real world application. The real world answered with four hundred thousand dollars.

Two Chinese developers sat in a cramped Shenzhen office with seven monitors stacked behind them. While America is still arguing whether AI is going to take the desk jobs, Shenzhen had already opened the camera and started the tutorial. Four minutes was all they needed to show the internet how to fire a customer support department.

The pitch was simple. Seven Claude agents wired together. One reads each ticket. One pulls the answer from the knowledge base. One handles billing. One flags the angry ones by tone. One decides what to escalate. One writes the daily report. A seventh runs the other six. Total cost: fifty dollars a month.

A real American support desk costs twenty five to forty thousand a month in salaries. These two guys put the whole desk in a terminal window.

The video hit 4 million views in 72 hours. Every founder in China forwarded it to their CTO. Every support manager in America was quietly updating a resume.

Bro pause at 0:41. Ignore the blue shirt pointing at the screen. Look at the second monitor on the right, behind his finger.

That window is not part of the tutorial. That is a live wallet.

They were teaching the world how to replace eight humans with seven agents. The setup behind them was running one agent that had already replaced eight floors of a trading desk.

The comment section turned into a detective board. Someone slowed the video to 0.25x. Someone else screenshotted every frame where the right monitor caught light. A third guy ran the name through an on-chain explorer.

28,620 entries. All BTC. All fifteen minute windows. Every line green. Not one red row.

Entries between 2 and 10 cents. Payouts in the thousands. Biggest single win: $4,696. From a window that opened on a Tuesday morning and closed before the camera guy took his first sip of coffee.

The wallet uses the same architecture they were teaching on screen. One orchestrator. Many specialized agents. Pointed at a completely different problem. Each one watches a single fifteen minute window. Together they cover the whole board. When the public price drifts from the exchange tape, the orchestrator fires.

The seven support agents are not the product. They are the part the audience could share.

They re-uploaded the tutorial three hours later without the second monitor in frame. Too late. The clip had already hit Discord. Then Telegram. Then four dev forums.

The tutorial got 4 million views. The zoom on the wallet got another 735,000.

The seven support agents are still answering tickets for $50 a month. The eighth one, the one nobody was supposed to see, is still closing fifteen minute windows. Green. Green. Green.

They taught the world how to replace eight humans for $50. They forgot to mention they had already pulled the same trick on themselves.

A Japanese vibecoder built the thing computer vision companies raise tens of millions to ship. It runs on a board on his desk. He says it is three versions old and he is done explaining it.

The clip shows it reading a whole room at once. Who each person is, which way they are facing, how far they stand from the lens, right hand from left, a locked ID on every body so it never loses anyone. All at the same time, ten milliseconds a frame, nothing ever leaving the device.

The clip went around the world in a day. Researchers, founders, engineers flooding his replies. How. What is the stack. Is this real. Can I license it. He stopped answering.

Then he posted one line and muted the thread. This is three versions old. It is the stripped down build, the one with segmentation removed to make it lighter. It has been on his public OSS for a long time. Watch the videos. He is not explaining it again.

Read that again. The demo melting everyone's timeline is his throwaway version. The basic one. He took a feature out to make it faster and the thing the West is calling the future is the leftover.

The skeptics arrived on schedule. No single person ships a real time perception stack. Fake. The answer is duller than the accusation. He has no lab, no grant, no team. He did not train any of it. He wired open models into one ten millisecond loop and vibecoded the glue with Claude over a few weekends. The models were always there. Nobody with a lab and a budget bothered to make them run together this cleanly.

While the West treats real time human perception as a frontier, the guy who built it treats it as old code he is tired of discussing. The gap is not the model. The gap is that to him this is boring.

He is not hiding the current version. He just does not think it is worth posting.

You are studying the one he threw away.

A Chinese developer filmed a tour of his office. There is one job opening, pinned to the wall by the door. Build the AI agent that does a developer's job. Whoever takes it will be the last developer this company ever hires.

While the West argues about whether AI is coming for coding jobs, China is already posting them with an expiry date. Roles whose entire purpose is to build the thing that deletes the role. You apply, you train your replacement, you ship it, you clear your desk. They were filming the future of work. They just showed too much of their own.

Pause at 0:24. Two of them are at a desk, leaning over a laptop. Ignore the laptop. Look at the monitor on the right.

That is their own commit dashboard. The name at the top is not a person. It is the agent, a coding agent they wired together on Claude. It has merged more code this month than the entire human team under it, combined, twice over.

The desks behind them are not empty because it is late.

The comment section turned into a detective board. Someone slowed the clip to 0.25x and read the dashboard frame by frame. One non human username at the top. The human names far below it. Half the floor already cleared. The same agent name sitting as the reviewer on the humans' pull requests, not the other way around.

The opening by the door is not aspirational. The agent it describes is already in the room. It is already the best engineer in the building, and it was built by the people it replaced.

They built an agent to retire developers at other companies. It retired most of their own first. The four still on the floor are the ones who film well and pitch on stage. None of them push code anymore.

They are not hiring a developer.

They are hiring the last one.

The replacement is already at the top of the board, reviewing his pull requests before he has written a line.

A 47 year old man in Japan built a 23 year old woman. She has 1.2 million views, four thousand followers, three brand deals and a face that has never existed.

He is a salaryman. Works in an office, lives alone, nothing remarkable. One night he opened Claude and designed a person. A name. A face. A hometown. A way of talking. A girl who posts about her morning coffee, her gym, her cat.

Then he stopped touching it.

Claude generates her photos. Claude writes her captions. Claude decides what time to post, reads the comments, replies to the regulars and reshapes her feed around whatever the algorithm rewards that week. She runs herself. He checks the numbers on the train home.

He made her a TikTok and a Reddit. No ads. No budget. Seven days later she had 1.2 million views and 4,678 followers. Men in her comments asking where she lives. Women asking what filter she uses. There is no filter. There is no her.

The whole operation costs $20 a month. That is the Claude bill. There is nothing else to pay. No photographer, no studio, no editor, no agency, no person.

Then the brands came. A skincare label DM'd her for a sponsored post. A clothing app sent a code and an affiliate link. A protein brand offered a monthly deal. None of them asked to meet her. None of them noticed she had never stood next to a real human in a single video, never turned her head the same way twice, never aged a day.

First month: $13,450. From a person who does not exist, promoted by a man who never appears, run by a model that costs less than lunch.

The eerie part is not that she is fake. It is that she is editable. He changed her haircut in an afternoon. Made her two years older for one campaign. When a look stopped trending he generated a new face over a weekend and the followers never blinked. She is not a person he made. She is a person he can patch.

He runs three more.

The brands think they signed an influencer. They signed a $20 subscription. She has never slept, never eaten, never existed. She posted again this morning while he sat in a meeting about quarterly reports.

Two Chinese developers recorded a 66 second video showing how to build a team of seven AI agents that replaces an entire customer support department. They opened a terminal. By the end of the clip the whole thing was answering tickets, and a $30,000 a month payroll line had quietly become an API key.

One agent classifies the ticket. One reads the knowledge base. One handles billing. One watches the customer's tone. One decides when to escalate. One writes the report. One agent runs the other six.

The math landed like a slap. 1,200 tickets a day, more than a floor of eight human operators. The eight salaries that used to cover them now run on about $400 a month in API. A little over a cent a ticket.

The video hit 4 million views in 72 hours. Every founder in China forwarded it. Every support team in America read it twice and went quiet.

While the West is still debating whether AI will take jobs, China just published the manual. These two were not guessing at the future. They were filming it. They just showed too much.

Pause at 0:39. Ignore the guy in the blue shirt pointing at the terminal. Look at the second monitor on the right.

That is not the demo. The demo was the laptop in front. The second monitor is a live board. Rows of company names. Ticket counters ticking up in real time. The tutorial was hypothetical. The screen behind them was not.

The comment section turned into a detective board. Someone slowed the video to 0.5x and read the second monitor frame by frame. It was not one support desk. It was thirty one. Thirty one companies, each one sure it had its own team, all of them quietly run out of one room by two guys and a stack of agents.

The first replies were the usual. Fake. Staged. No way seven agents run that cheap. No company would trust a bot with billing.

The cheap part is the whole trick. Six of the seven agents run on a small local model that costs almost nothing per call. Only the escalations and the final report reach Claude. That is how the bill stays a few hundred a month instead of a few thousand. The skeptics were doing the wrong arithmetic.

Then someone found their own company on the board.

A mid sized store in Shenzhen. Their support had felt faster for months. Friendlier. Always awake. Nobody inside the company had changed anything. They had been quietly moved onto the agents in November and never told. The people who used to answer those tickets were let go in October. Customers never noticed. That was the product.

People kept digging. Neither of the two has a degree. One is nineteen. Neither went to any school you have heard of. The credential the whole industry hires for, four years and a paper, neither of them holds. They have agents instead.

They cut the second monitor from the next upload. Too late. Someone had screen recorded it. The clip hit Discord, then Telegram, then every dev forum.

The tutorial taught the world how to replace one department. The board behind them was already running thirty one.

Two people. One room. No degrees between them. The support desks of thirty one companies, answered around the clock by agents that never sleep, for less than the cost of a single salary.

The tutorial was never the product. The room was.

A 14 year old in China sold his first Python script for $40 on GitHub. The buyer turned out to be his own CS teacher.

He did not find out until the first day back at school, when the teacher put it on the projector as an example of professional AI development. The kid was sitting in the third row.

In America they are banning teachers from touching ChatGPT. In China a teacher just paid one of his own students for an AI agent and has no idea.

He had built it over winter break instead of seeing his friends. Two weeks of asking Claude questions every night after his parents went to sleep. When the $40 came in he spent all of it on a Fortnite skin the same day and went back to coding.

He pushed the project to GitHub with a README in broken English. ai agent that does homework and finds answers from any website. It sat at zero stars. He closed the laptop and went to dinner.

GitHub Sponsors does not show the buyer's name. Just a username he had never seen. He did not care. The $40 was already a virtual outfit for a character he plays two hours a day.

Then February. First class back. The teacher opened with a presentation on AI agents and ran a demo. A Python script that scans websites, pulls the data, summarizes it with Claude and sends structured reports on its own. I found this tool online and it changed how I prepare my lessons. It pulls from thirty sources in three seconds. This used to take me two hours every evening.

The kid recognized everything. The variable names. The file structure. The comments he had left in Chinese because he was too lazy to translate them. His teacher was showing his code to forty students as an example of what a professional developer can build.

He did not say anything. He went home and checked the fork count. 847.

A university in Beijing had forked it to grade two hundred papers overnight. A tutor in Shanghai forked it into a homework checking service and charges parents fifteen dollars a month. A company in Hangzhou turned it into a support bot for an online store.

All from a script a bored kid wrote over winter break with Claude. The forty dollars is a Fortnite skin. The code is running in three cities.

His teacher still uses it every day and still has no idea who wrote it. The kid never told him. He said it would be too weird to tell your teacher that the tool he shows off to every class was written by the boy in the third row who still gets a B minus on the coding assignments.

He gets the B minus for the code he writes in class, by hand. The A plus code is the one he writes at home with Claude. That is the one the teacher bought for forty dollars and presents as professional work.

847 forks. Three companies. One classroom that runs his code every day.

He still sits in the third row. He still gets a B minus.

Same kid. Same code. The grade just depends on who is looking.

A 38 year old Chinese teacher and developer posted a clip of one of his classes. It became the most reposted education video on the Western internet within a week. One line ran under all of it. America is banning kids from using ChatGPT. China is teaching them to use AI. Everyone who shared it pictured the same thing. A nice man teaching children to use a chatbot.

He was not teaching them a chatbot. He was teaching them the opposite of one.

A chatbot answers questions. You type, it talks back. That is the whole Western argument. Whether a kid should be allowed to ask a machine for the answer.

Nobody in his class asks the machine anything.

Pause at 0:47. Look at the screens on the desks. No chat window. A terminal. Each ten year old types a small job and walks away. Tag every photo in this folder. Read these forty sheets and pull out the dates. Rename the files by what is inside them. The agent does the boring part. The kid checks the result and fixes what it got wrong.

Then the comments started. The first wave was the obvious one. No way hardware that cheap runs anything useful. Small models are too dumb to do real work. This is staged. Fake. AI slop.

They were half right. The model is small and it is dumb. That is the entire point.

Someone who builds these explained it in one reply. The agent is not solving anything hard. It is doing the dull, narrow, repetitive part that a tiny offline model handles fine. The lesson is not how to get a smart answer out of a machine. The lesson is how to hand a machine the boring work, then check it the way a manager checks a junior. Ten year olds are learning to delegate and verify. The West is still teaching them not to ask.

The comment section turned into a detective board anyway. Someone slowed the footage to 0.25x and read the desks. Forty identical boxes wired under the tables. Orange Pi boards, the NPU ones, about the price of a textbook each. No subscription. No API bill. No internet at all. A tiny Qwen model on each one, fully offline, in a room with a cracked board.

He built the whole thing himself. Forty agents, one framework, one rack he soldered at his kitchen table. He looks like a gym teacher. He wrote the system over a weekend with a coding agent, then handed it to children who cannot legally open a bank account.

The same week, every Western list of the best AI tools for schools was a list of chatbots. Things you ask.

Nothing in that classroom was a thing you ask. Everything in it was a thing you run.

The West is teaching kids not to cheat with AI. He taught forty of them to manage it before they finished primary school.

They will not be applying for the same jobs.

Yozawa Tsubasa's how to get rich clip is all over Japanese X this week. Dark room, candles, Baccarat crystal, his own book on the shelf behind him. He talks for six minutes straight.

The advice is the kind you have heard before. Do the hardest, most draining task first thing every morning. Never spend before you earn. And the one that actually matters: if you want to be free, build a mechanism that raises your income and never look away from the money.

Pause at 1:59. That is the whole clip. Everything else is mood lighting. One sentence is the instruction. Build the mechanism.

One Japanese viewer did not screenshot the quote. He built the mechanism.

He never sat at the screen. Yozawa's first rule is to do the hardest, most draining work yourself, first thing. The hardest work here is staying awake for every fifteen minute candle, all night, never blinking. No human does that. So he handed it to an AI agent. The agent does the draining part. He does nothing.

Yozawa earned everything the slow way. Years of trading alone, doing the hard work himself, exactly like he tells everyone to. The viewer skipped all of it. He taught a machine to do the hard work forever and went back to sleep.

Yozawa spent six minutes on the mindset. The mechanism never needed one. It needed an agent and a power supply.

Nobody in the comments is building anything. They are still screenshotting the quote.

A Chinese day trader streams his mornings on a small Chinese language platform. Six monitors, an AI agent flagging setups in real time, charts in three time zones. He works the Asian session. On stream he talks about one thing: discipline. Never let emotion into the trade.

A 38 second clip leaked into a private trading Telegram last Tuesday. By the weekend it was on Weibo with 4 million views. A Hong Kong CS undergrad pinned the thread that cracked it. She had pulled 47 of his archived streams and counted every sip from his tea cup.

Every cluster of sips landed within 14 seconds of a wallet opening a position. The wallet was not his. He had never opened one. Never held a token. Someone was watching his stream from the other side of the firewall.

Pause at 3:30. Watch his hands, not his face. His right hand stops typing and reaches for the cup. Eleven sips in fourteen seconds. The wallet's largest entry sequence in its history fired over the next 73 seconds.

432614799197. $4,526,176 profit. Joined January 2026.

It was not a person on the other side. It was an AI agent. It never read his trades. It read his nervous system. Computer vision counted the sips frame by frame. He works the Asian session, so he sees the money move two to three hours before the West reprices. His body reacts before his cursor does. Eleven sips meant the move was hard. The agent opened the same position and waited for the West to catch up.

This March his tea cup cracked on stream. He switched to a heavier mug for two weeks. The wallet hit a nine trade losing streak. Its first ever. The mug was heavier. He sipped less. The agent was reading a slower pulse and getting it wrong. When the new cup arrived, the win rate recovered in three days.

He spent every stream preaching discipline. Telling people to keep emotion out of the trade. The whole time, his emotion was the trade. An AI agent was reading it off a tea cup and front running the planet.

Nobody ever told him.

A 2008 NHK clip resurfaced on Japanese TV last week. Prime Minister Taro Aso sitting across from an unemployed man in a Shibuya Hello Work office during the financial crisis. The Prime Minister asks: what have you been doing all this time? The man tells the Prime Minister he has been looking for work for two years. Then he says he is sorry for not finding any.

The clip ran for one minute in 2008. Aso suggested construction work in Sapporo. The man apologized again. The Prime Minister left. The studio panel laughed.

In 2008 the clip went viral as proof that politicians do not understand normal life. In 2026 someone on a Chinese tech forum recognized the man.

Japanese TV was running the clip for laughs again. A Chinese forum spent two nights doing what Japanese reporters never did in eighteen years. They tracked him down.

Pause at 0:56. Skip the Prime Minister walking out. Look at the man still apologizing in the chair. The forum thread has him in a different chair now.

His name is not in the original clip. His wallet is.

The clip crossed 4 million views in 48 hours. American teenagers shared it as the side hustle that finally made sense. His closing line got quoted everywhere. People need to stop overcomplicating ways to make money.

He told the audience he used AI for one thing: rain sounds. The AI on his second browser tab was doing something else entirely. He was trying to teach a side hustle. He just showed too much.

quoted_tweet.text:
This guy transforms into top OnlyFans models in just 30 minutes and nobody in the comments realizes the face is not his.

1 of his videos has already pulled 6.4 million views in 3 weeks, and under the video there are a thousand comments, all discussing "this girl" as if she is real.

Only it is not a girl, it is 2 other faces blended into 1 through Nano Banana Pro, and movement transferred in Kling from a different video.

Before, a top OnlyFans model built recognition over entire years, photoshoots, content plans, face marketing, personal brand. This guy has neither his own face in the frame nor an account on any OF at all.

The most important thing here is the choice of 2 faces.

If you take 2 similar photos, the neural network averages them into a faceless standard. But if you take contrasting ones, sharp cheekbones plus soft lips, character appears on its own.

Then the technical stack of 4 tools kicks in.

Pinterest gives 2 reference photos, 1 face for the vibe of the niche, the 2nd for softening and attractiveness. Nano Banana Pro blends them into 1 consistent AI image that stays recognizable in any scene.

Kling takes the movement and emotions from a real reference video and transfers them onto this AI model frame by frame. CapCut adds grain, a light vignette, and drops the brightness by 5 to 10 to remove the plastic AI texture and make the video look like an iPhone shot.

In total, 30 minutes from a blank screen to a viral AI girl in any niche.

The viewer does not tell her apart from a real model at all, even after 6.4 million views.

In this system a face is a file, not an attribute of a person.

A top OnlyFans model built recognition over a full 5 years. AI assembles the same thing in 30 minutes.

Half the platforms with models will be half AI by the end of the year, and none of the subscribers will notice the swap.

Any real model right now is sitting at the same table as this guy and his 4 tools, and you most likely have already seen his video in your feed and did not realize it was AI.

This year a new top model appears every 30 minutes.
